description: Apollo 13 Flight Journal - Day 6, part 7: Farewell, Aquarius

tokens: The splashdown for Apollo 13 is two hours away.
The damaged Service Module is gone, and the long dormant Odyssey is slowly showing signs of life as they work to re-establish attitude control and navigation systems.
Very soon it will be time to separate from their long-serving lifeboat.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I have an RCS light, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: That's the one we warned you about some time ago.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The helium pressure in the Command Module RCS is more than sufficient.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There's a star angle difference with stars 36 and 40.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Much to everyone's delight, Jack's star measurements appear to be successful.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Copy you're maneuvering to the LM Jett attitude.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'm going to torque those gimbal angles.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Torque them, babe, full stroke gimbal.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Jack proceeds with the fine alignment of the IMU platform by torqueing the gimbals, much like he has done at every P52 throughout the mission, but none have been performed under such extraordinary circumstances.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're suddenly ahead of the timeline.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I did a star check, and it passes.
It puts that star right in the telescope.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This was the eighth realignment of the Command Module guidance platform, and Jack's seventh and final P52.
As a reference, he sighted on star 36 Vega (Alpha Lyrae) and star 40 Altair (Alpha Aquilae).
The star angle difference measured was 'all ball's or '00000' on the display, meaning that his final, hurried P52 was one of perfect accuracy within the limits of the system.
He performed a sighting of a another star to check the alignment but this star is not recorded in the Mission Report.
Finally, he torqued the gimbals for correct fine alignment.
The angles were were -1.253° in X, 0.365° in Y and 3.263° in Z.
The platform was torqued at 140:52:00 GET.
This aligns their IMU to the Entry REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can press on in the checklist, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Mind out LM gimbal lock, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: It's the Command Module's gimbal lock I'm worried about.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 140 hours, 55 minutes into the flight.
That star check report confirms proper alignment of the Command Module platform.
We are 45 minutes away now from scheduled time of LM jettison.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: If you concur, we would be inclined to recommend that you go to AGS for the maneuver because you'll have to be there later on anyway.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And AGS Pulse is the recommendation.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We're having trouble maneuvering, Joe, without getting it in gimbal lock.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You picked a lousy attitude, though, to separate.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Aah, Houston, why can't I stay in PGNS Att Hold for the LM attitude hold?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Our recommendation is that you stay in AGS for the LM Jett simply because we've thought it through.
We've got the right deadbands and all set up.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: It's a lot easier for me to maneuver in PGNS, Joe, than it is AGS.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You can maneuver in PGNS, Jim.
I'm sorry, I thought you were talking about the Att Hold and the LM Jett afterward.
You can maneuver in PGNS if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Velocity now reading 15,192 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo is coming in close now at 29,235 kilometers from Earth, and speeding up to 4,630 metres/second.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I want to get way over here, Joe.
To prevent him into gimbal lock, I have the yaw at about - I'd say about almost 50 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Just stay out of gimbal lock and that 45-degree isn't critical - the out of plane, that is.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The particular DAP configuration we have set up now is using more fuel than we'd like out of system B.
We'd like you to reconfigure the DAP to 30021.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The final DAP configuration is Docked, 2 Jet/RCS A, fine scale, 5-degree deadband, and .5-degree rate.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like to have you turn the optics power off until you need them again, and we would like to have you check the RCS ring 1 and 2 temps for us.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The optics draw 3.1 amps of power, not an inconsiderable amount under the circumstances.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our ring 1 is 50; ring 2 is plus 42.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Ring 1 temperature is 10°C, while Ring 2 shows 5.6°C.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I am at the LM Sep attitude and I'm planning on bailing out.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I can't think of a better idea, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'll go to AGS Att Hold, if you want me to.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: In AGS Att Hold, we recommend Wide Dead Band.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'm in the AGS Dead Band.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You need to relay something downstairs, Jim?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Our troops would like to have you go to the Systems Test Meter and read out the injector temps on RCS for us.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Let me read out the injector temperatures for you.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: 5 Charlie, 4.2; 5 Dogs 4.6; 6 Alpha, 4.3; 6 Bravo, 4.3; 6 Charlie, 3.6; 6 Dog, 3.6.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We're ready to proceed with hatch closeup.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Did Jim get the film out of Aquarius?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We - You mean the film we took this morning?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, let me mention something about the hatch integrity check.
You're going to vent the tunnel until you get a 3-psi Delta-P.
That should take 9 or 10 minutes, and it's our firm feeling that you don't have to wait another 10 minutes after that for a leak check.
If it holds pressure for a minute or so or even gets down there, you know you've got a good hatch.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 141 hours, 8 minutes now into the flight.
Apollo 13 presently 14,468 nautical miles out from Earth.
All three crewmen now in the Command Module preparing to install the hatch.
We show 32 minutes, 40 seconds away from scheduled time of Lunar Module jettison.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; now 141 hours, 11 minutes now into the mission.
It's less than 30 minutes away from the scheduled time of jettisoning the Lunar Module.
We show Apollo 13 at an altitude of 14,020 nautical miles.
We have an estimate of an altitude of 9,729 nautical miles at the time of jettisoning the Lunar Module.
We're at 141 hours, 11 minutes; continuing to monitor.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Their distance is 25,965 km, and closing very fast.
They expect an approximate distance of 18,000 km at the time of LM separation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They have closed the hatch into the LM.
A warning light has come up, alerting the crew that oxygen is flowing out of the CM's own supply at an unusually high rate.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, we might be pressurizing a lot of the system that was not pressurized.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Take a good look at Cabin.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Just verify your Direct O2is Off.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: A valve on Jack's left hand console allows him to flow in oxygen into the cabin under manual control.
This valve is not the reason for their alarm, though.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We think that you were just pressurizing the cabin up to that regulator's particular spec.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: With the CM top hatch closed, their life support system needs to take over the full responsibility for maintaining the cabin atmosphere.
It appeared to them from the instruments that the oxygen flow was higher than expected.
They don't find it a problem, instead thinking that the system is making up for any difference it detects.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're observing middle gimbal angle getting a little bit high.
The LM appears to be deadbanding okay within its attitude, but just wanted you to know that we're keeping an eye on it and if it does get too high, we might want you to punch off early.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Joe is expressing worry that the Lunar Module, now left to its own devices under autopilot control, might accidentally move the Command Module to an attitude that threatens them with the gimbal lock.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And verify that the hatch is secured and that you are venting the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 141 hours, 16 minutes into the flight.
Apollo 13 now 13,283 nautical miles away from Earth with a velocity building up now to 16,350 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Since we're ahead of the time line, can I proceed on and kind of punch off early, or do you want me to punch off at exactly 1 hour?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, when you are comfortably ready to punch off, you can go ahead and do it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That was Jack Swigert receiving that confirmation from the ground from Joe Kerwin that he can jettison the Lunar Module at his convenience.
So we'll stand by for that possibility at any time.
We're at 141 hours, 17 minutes into the flight of Apollo 13.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We just had a formal Go for LM Jett at your convenience.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We're ready for you to bring the BMAGs On and Warm Up and all other things being equal, we'd like you to go through the LM Jett at - on the checklist you've got.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That's BMAG 1 now, as you know.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Yes, BMAG 1 is in Warm Up.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The BMAGs will not only provide them with attitude information for their FDAI 8-ball display to support the info provided by the computer, but will also take over as the main source of attitude data should the IMU suddenly fail.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 141 hours, 20 minutes now into the flight.
While we're standing by, we'll pass along some updated numbers from the Retrofire Officer.
We show time of entry in the Earth's atmosphere at 142 hours, 40 minutes, 39 seconds.
Blackout begins at plus 18 seconds from time of Entry Interface...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Do you have a Command Module weight for me?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Blackout ends at 3 minutes, 23 seconds from time of Entry Interface.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'm reminded that the entry DAP is all you've got.
It'll initialize itself, and you won't have an - you won't have an RCS DAP, so you don't have to fool with it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Main chutes deploy at 9 minutes, 4 seconds from time of Entry Interface.
Predicted time of splash; 14 minutes, 1 second from time of Entry Interface.
Standing by, continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; now 141 hours, 22 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 presently at an altitude of 12,474 nautical miles, at a speed of 16,761 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Distance to go, 23,102 km; speed, 5,109 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm - Have you got your checklist out there on the SCS powerup?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'm coming down here to a step that says FDAI Power, off.
Is that what you want right now, I guess, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That FDAI Power, Off, is to stop momentary glitches as you bring up the BMAG to put it on, and you turn right around and put the FDAI Power right back on 1.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Since the FDAI's draw the rate information from the BMAGs, turning the BMAGs on while the FDAI's are working might cause them to give false information.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I don't have the BMAG Temp light out yet.
Do you want me to go ahead and put the BMAG On with the Temp light still On?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We can go ahead and you'll have a good stable rate.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 141 hours, 24 minutes now into the flight.

The flight surgeon's now monitoring the consoles are taking readings on Jack Swigert and Fred Haise's heart rates on these gentlemen just running over 100, slightly higher than average indicating an increased work load.
We now show Apollo 13 at 12,064 nautical miles away from Earth, speed continuing to build up, now reading 16,989 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Distance to go is 22,343 km speed, 5,178 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It appears the crew has put on their biomedical harnesses again and plugged them in to send the biomedical telemetry to Houston for the rest of the mission, after having removed them earlier during the coast.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That was Jack Swigert indicating that they plan to jettison the Lunar Module in about 3 minutes from this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 141 hours, 28 minutes into the flight.
Apollo 13 now 11,590 nautical miles away from Earth.
For reentry, the 3 crewmen will be traveling in shirt sleeve environment, not in their space suits.
We're standing by now for reports of jettison of the Lunar Module.
At 141 hours, 28 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Do we have a Go for Pyro Arm?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We can give you a Go if you'll put the Logic On momentarily first.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Just copied that, and you are Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Farewell, Aquarius, and we thank you.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Joe's warning is not without cause.
Their maneuvering has put them perilously close to the state that would make them lose their recently acquired guidance reference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Swigert, from1970 Technical debrief:"On the CM/LM Sep, the LM moved smartly away and the noise didn't appear to be excessive.
Immediately, I noticed a pitchup in the CM about the same time that Jim called a Gimbal Lock.
We were sitting right near the gimbal lock limits, and he had a Gimbal Lock light on the DSKY status lights.
I pitched down, using the Direct RCS switches.
I came off then with the three Manual Attitude switches.
We did get out of the gimbal lock region and stabilized.
All the time I noticed a continual pitchup rate.
However minor, it would definitely affect the attitudes, continually pitched up.
We went from the separation attitude down to the entry attitude, and Jim performed the sextant star check.
Our maneuver from that was to the Moon-check attitude, and we maintained this attitude in a kind of wide deadband fashion until our Moon-check time."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"Maybe it was because of your being busy with the test , whereas I was just an innocent bystander sitting over there , but the LM Sep impressed me as being the loudest pyro event that I heard from stem to stern during the mission."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"It was encouraging, I know."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"It was very close, and it impressed the heck out of me, I know.
It actually rocked me off my seat toward the window when it let go."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Swigert, from1970 Technical debrief:"I didn't notice that, perhaps because I had both hands at the controls."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 141 hours, 31 minutes into the flight.
We have had Lunar Module jettison, and for Apollo 13, the age of Aquarius ended at 141 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
transcript.speaker: PAO
